quiz_questions.question_text: What type of image does a convex mirror always form?

options.text: Virtual, upright, and diminished
Real and inverted
Virtual and enlarged
Real and upright

quiz_questions.explanation: Convex mirror — Diverging — always forms virtual, upright, diminished images. Key Concepts — Lenses & Mirrors Mirrors Concave mirror : Converging — forms real (inverted) or virtual (upright, magnified) images. The other options — 'Real and inverted', 'Virtual and enlarged', and 'Real and upright' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the mirror/lens equation.

options.text: f = d_o + d_i
f = d_o × d_i

quiz_questions.explanation: Thin lens equation — 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i (same form as mirror equation). The other options — 'f = d_o + d_i', 'f = d_o × d_i', and '1/f = 1/d_o − 1/d_i' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the magnification formula?

options.text: m = d_i + d_o
m = f/d_o
m = d_o/d_i
m = −d_i/d_o

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnification m = −d_i/d_o gives the ratio of image height to object height. A negative m means the image is inverted. The other options are incorrect formulations of the magnification relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: If |m| > 1, the image is:

options.text: Enlarged
Virtual
Same size
Diminished

quiz_questions.explanation: When the absolute value of magnification is greater than 1, the image is larger than the object. When |m| < 1, the image is diminished; when |m| = 1, they are the same size.

quiz_questions.question_text: A diverging lens always forms:

options.text: Virtual, upright, diminished image
Virtual, inverted image
Real, upright image
Real, inverted, enlarged image

quiz_questions.explanation: A diverging (concave) lens always forms virtual, upright, diminished images, regardless of where the object is placed. This is similar to a convex mirror.

quiz_questions.question_text: A convex (converging) lens has what kind of focal length?

options.text: Zero
Infinite
Positive
Negative

quiz_questions.explanation: Lenses Convex (converging) lens : Positive focal length. Concave (diverging) lens : Negative focal length.

quiz_questions.question_text: If m is negative, the image is:

options.text: Upright
Virtual
Larger
Inverted

quiz_questions.explanation: A negative magnification means the image is inverted (flipped upside down). Real images formed by converging lenses and concave mirrors are inverted when the object is beyond the focal point.

quiz_questions.question_text: What kind of mirror is used in car side mirrors?

options.text: A flat plane mirror with no curvature
A convex mirror for a wider field of view
A concave mirror to magnify nearby objects
A two-way mirror for rear visibility only

quiz_questions.explanation: Car side mirrors use convex mirrors because they provide a wider field of view, allowing drivers to see more of the area behind them. The trade-off is that objects appear smaller and farther away.

quiz_questions.question_text: A concave mirror can form which types of images?

options.text: Only virtual and diminished images always
Only real and inverted images always
Both real and virtual images depending on position
Only virtual and inverted images always

quiz_questions.explanation: A concave mirror can form real, inverted images when the object is beyond the focal point, or virtual, upright, magnified images when the object is between the focal point and the mirror.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where is the focal point of a concave mirror?

options.text: At twice the distance of the center of curvature
Behind the mirror on the other reflective side
Halfway between the mirror and its center of curvature
At the exact surface of the mirror's front face

quiz_questions.explanation: The focal point of a concave mirror is located at half the radius of curvature (f = R/2), which is halfway between the mirror surface and the center of curvature.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'focal length' of a lens or mirror?

options.text: The distance between the lens and the image it forms
The distance from the lens or mirror to its focal point
The radius of curvature of the lens or mirror surface
The distance between the object and the lens or mirror

quiz_questions.explanation: Focal length is the distance from the center of a lens or the surface of a mirror to the focal point, where parallel light rays converge (converging) or appear to diverge from (diverging).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'converging' mean when describing a lens?

options.text: The lens spreads light rays apart as they pass through
The lens brings parallel light rays together to a point
The lens reflects light back toward the source direction
The lens absorbs certain wavelengths of visible light

quiz_questions.explanation: A converging lens (convex lens) refracts parallel light rays so they come together (converge) at the focal point on the other side. This is the opposite of a diverging lens, which spreads rays apart.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'virtual image' in optics?

options.text: An image that can be projected onto a physical screen
An image formed where light rays actually converge together
An image formed where light rays only appear to converge
An image that is always inverted and smaller than the object

quiz_questions.explanation: A virtual image is formed where light rays appear to diverge from but do not actually meet. It cannot be captured on a screen. Examples include images in flat mirrors and magnifying glasses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'real image' in optics?

options.text: An image that appears behind a flat mirror surface
An image that only exists in the observer's brain
An image that is always upright and larger than the object
An image formed where light rays actually meet and can be projected

quiz_questions.explanation: A real image is formed where actual light rays converge and intersect. Unlike virtual images, real images can be projected onto a screen. They are typically inverted.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'magnification' mean in optics?

options.text: The ratio of the image size to the original object size
The brightness of an image compared to the light source
The focal length divided by the object's total distance
The sharpness or clarity of the image being produced

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnification is the ratio of image height to object height (m = h_i/h_o = −d_i/d_o). A magnification greater than 1 means the image is larger than the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'center of curvature' refer to for a curved mirror?

options.text: The center of the sphere from which the mirror surface is cut
The exact midpoint of the mirror surface from edge to edge
The focal point where all reflected parallel rays converge
The point where the principal axis meets the mirror surface

quiz_questions.explanation: The center of curvature is the center of the sphere of which the curved mirror forms a part. The radius of curvature is the distance from this center to the mirror surface, and the focal length is half that distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define the 'principal axis' of a lens or mirror.

options.text: Any light ray that passes through the center of the lens
The straight line passing through the center and focal point
The curved path that light follows inside a glass lens
The boundary edge around the outer rim of the mirror

quiz_questions.explanation: The principal axis is the imaginary straight line that passes through the center of a lens or mirror and through its focal point. It serves as the reference line for ray diagrams.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'diverging' mean when referring to a lens or mirror?

options.text: It brings all parallel light rays together at one focal point
It causes parallel light rays to spread apart from each other
It absorbs all incoming light and converts it to heat energy
It blocks certain colors while allowing others to pass through

quiz_questions.explanation: A diverging optical element (concave lens or convex mirror) causes parallel light rays to spread apart. The rays appear to come from a virtual focal point behind the lens or mirror.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'object distance' in a lens or mirror problem?

options.text: The distance from the lens or mirror to the object being viewed
The distance from the focal point to the center of curvature
The distance from the image to the focal point of the lens
The total thickness of the lens from front face to back face

quiz_questions.explanation: Object distance (d_o) is the distance from the object to the lens or mirror surface. It is one of the key variables in the mirror/lens equation 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'image distance' mean in optics?

options.text: The distance from the object to its reflected or refracted image
The distance from the lens or mirror surface to the image formed
The total distance light travels from the source to the screen
The distance between the two focal points of a thick lens

quiz_questions.explanation: Image distance (d_i) is the distance from the lens or mirror to the location where the image forms. A positive d_i indicates a real image on the opposite side; negative means virtual.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student holds a magnifying glass close to a page and sees enlarged text. What type of image is being formed?

options.text: A real, inverted, and diminished image of the text
A virtual, upright, and magnified image of the text
A real, upright, and magnified image of the text
A virtual, inverted, and diminished image of the text

quiz_questions.explanation: When an object is placed between a converging lens and its focal point, the lens acts as a magnifying glass. The image formed is virtual (cannot be projected), upright, and magnified.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks into a spoon's inner (concave) surface and sees an upside-down image of their face. Where must their face be relative to the focal point?

options.text: Their face must be between the spoon and the focal point
Their face must be beyond the focal point of the spoon
Their face must be exactly at the focal point of the spoon
Their face must be behind the spoon's reflective surface

quiz_questions.explanation: A concave mirror forms a real, inverted image when the object is beyond the focal point. If the student sees an upside-down reflection, their face must be farther away than the focal length of the spoon.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student places an object at the focal point of a converging lens. What happens to the image?

options.text: The image forms at twice the focal length away from the lens
No image forms because the refracted rays travel in parallel
A small virtual image appears between the object and lens
A real inverted image forms right at the focal point itself

quiz_questions.explanation: When an object is placed exactly at the focal point of a converging lens, the refracted rays emerge parallel to each other and never converge. No image is formed at any finite distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses a concave mirror as a makeup mirror. Why does their face appear larger?

options.text: Concave mirrors always form enlarged real inverted images
The face is between the mirror and its focal point, forming a magnified virtual image
Concave mirrors have the same effect as a magnifying glass lens
The curved surface stretches the reflected light across more area

quiz_questions.explanation: When your face is closer to a concave mirror than its focal point, the mirror forms a virtual, upright, magnified image. This is why concave mirrors are used as makeup and shaving mirrors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that a security mirror in a store shows a wide-angle view. What type of mirror is it, and why?

options.text: A concave mirror because it focuses light into a narrow beam
A flat mirror because it reflects light without any distortion
A convex mirror because it diverges light and gives a wider view
A two-way mirror because it lets security see through the glass

quiz_questions.explanation: Security mirrors are convex mirrors. They diverge reflected light, providing a wider field of view so that more of the store can be monitored. The images appear smaller but cover a larger area.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a converging lens produces a real image on a screen. A student moves the object closer to the lens. What happens to the image?

options.text: The image moves closer to the lens and gets smaller in size
The image stays in the same position but becomes brighter
The image moves farther from the lens and gets larger in size
The image disappears completely from the projection screen

quiz_questions.explanation: When the object moves closer to a converging lens (but remains beyond the focal point), the image distance increases and the image becomes larger. This follows from the lens equation 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wears glasses with concave (diverging) lenses. What vision problem do they most likely have?

options.text: Farsightedness, where nearby objects appear blurry to them
Nearsightedness, where distant objects appear blurry to them
Astigmatism, where all objects appear distorted at any distance
Color blindness, where certain colors cannot be distinguished

quiz_questions.explanation: Diverging (concave) lenses are used to correct myopia (nearsightedness). In myopia, the eye focuses images in front of the retina. The diverging lens spreads light slightly before it enters the eye, moving the focal point back onto the retina.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sets up a concave mirror and finds that the image is the same size as the object. Where is the object placed?

options.text: At the focal point of the concave mirror surface
At the center of curvature of the concave mirror
Very far from the mirror at an infinite distance away
Between the focal point and the mirror surface itself

quiz_questions.explanation: When an object is placed at the center of curvature (2f) of a concave mirror, the image forms at the same distance and is the same size as the object (|m| = 1), but inverted.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses a projector that contains a converging lens. The lens creates a large image on a distant screen. What type of image is this?

options.text: A virtual upright image that cannot be shown on a screen
A real inverted image that is projected onto the screen surface
A virtual inverted image visible only when looking through the lens
A real upright image formed by total internal reflection in the lens

quiz_questions.explanation: A projector uses a converging lens to produce a real, inverted, enlarged image on a screen. The slide or image source is placed slightly beyond the focal point, and the projector flips the source so the projected image appears right-side up.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student calculates magnification m = −2 for an image formed by a lens. What does this tell them about the image?

options.text: The image is upright and half the size of the original object
The image is inverted and twice the size of the original object
The image is virtual and exactly the same size as the object
The image is upright and twice the size of the original object

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnification of m = −2 means the image is inverted (negative sign) and twice the size of the object (|m| = 2). The negative sign always indicates an inverted image in optics sign conventions.
